figures.caption: Fig. 3. Planar SLIP sweep showing mechanism transfer from 1D. Left. Primary D2D normalized CoM mismatch. Center. Secondary stance-timing deviation. Both exhibit a monotonic trend as α decreases, with 15◦ (red) and 20◦ (orange) curves nearly superposed, indicating the primary mechanism is angle-insensitive in this range. Right. Tertiary friction ratio |Fh|/Fv , essentially flat in α and sitting near tan(θTD) for each touchdown angle. The dashed red line marks the friction cone at µ = 0.7. The 30◦ spot check (stars on all three panels) confirms transfer into the aggressive-geometry regime.

Fig. 2. Empirical 50 percent crossing α50 versus predicted αcrit from (17) across ten parameter combinations. Log-log fit yields R2 = 0.986 with a consistent proportionality α50 ≈ 0.67αcrit. Points lie systematically below the diagonal because αcrit marks the onset of infeasibility, while α50 measures a performance threshold reached after deviation accumulates.
TABLE I NOTATION USED THROUGHOUT THE PAPER.

Fig. 4. Conservative tuning and structural reach. (A) Conservatism curve ∆k′(α)/∆k with three shaded regimes. The infeasible regime is α < αinfeas, the conservative-tuning regime is αinfeas ≤ α < αcrit, and no restriction is needed for α ≥ αcrit. The curve saturates at unity for α ≥ αcrit, indicating conservative tuning is unnecessary there. (B) Conditional cost J/Jideal for unrestricted parameter-based (orange), conservative parameter-based (red), and stiffness-as-state (blue). In the overlap regime all three sit within approximately 10 percent of one another. (C) Reach as horizontal bars. Stiffness-as-state covers the full range. Conservative parameter-based terminates at αinfeas. Unrestricted parameter-based is guaranteed only for α ≥ αcrit. Restricting the admissible stiffness range preserves realizability at the cost of reach, not cost.
